Understanding Palantir’s Ontology: Semantic, Kinetic, and Dynamic Layers Explained
Cristian Caruso
3 min read
·
Jun 13, 2025

--

4

If you’ve worked with Palantir Foundry or Gotham, you’ve likely heard the term ontology used again and again. In Palantir’s world, ontology is not just a buzzword, it’s the foundational concept that brings clarity, structure, and meaning to vast, complex data ecosystems.

But to truly understand how Palantir turns data into insight and action, you need to grasp the three core layers of the ontology: Semantic, Kinetic, and Dynamic.

Let’s break them down.

🔷 1. The Semantic Layer — What the World Is

The semantic layer is the core of the ontology. It defines the conceptual model of your domain , what entities exist, how they relate to one another, and what properties they have.

Think of it as answering the question:

“What are the things that matter in our world?”

Examples:
Entities: Person, Vehicle, Transaction, Organization
Relationships: A Person owns a Vehicle, a Vehicle is registered with an Organization
Properties: A Person has a name, a Transaction has a timestamp

The semantic layer lets you define a shared language across teams and data sources, reconciling fragmented data concepts like “user”, “client”, or “individual” into a unified Person entity.

🔷 2. The Kinetic Layer — Connecting to Reality

While the semantic layer defines meaning, the kinetic layer connects that meaning to actual data.

Get Cristian Caruso’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

This layer implements the ontology by linking real-world data sources, databases, APIs, files, into the conceptual model.

What it does:
Maps raw data (tables, fields) to ontological entities and relationships
Powers ETL (Extract-Transform-Load) pipelines
Enables traceable, auditable data lineage from source to insight
Example:
A SQL table tbl_customers with columns like first_name, last_name, id gets mapped to the Person entity
A CSV containing license plates maps into the Vehicle entity

The kinetic layer operationalizes your ontology, ensuring it’s fed with up-to-date, accurate information.

🔷 3. The Dynamic Layer — Making It Come Alive

The dynamic layer introduces behavior to the ontology. This is where business rules, policies, workflows, and permissions live.

What it includes:
Business rules (e.g., “A Person can only be assigned a case if they are active”)
Access control (e.g., users can only see entities linked to their department)
Lifecycle management (e.g., Suspect → Investigated → Cleared)

This layer ensures that the ontology is not just a model, but an active system that adapts, governs, and enforces logic across the platform.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
✅ Why It Matters in Palantir

In a Palantir environment:

Analysts work within the semantic model to explore and derive insights
Engineers build out the kinetic layer to integrate and normalize data
End users interact through apps and dashboards powered by the dynamic layer

This structure lets you scale understanding and control, across massive datasets and complex operations, without losing clarity.

🎯 Final Thoughts

Palantir’s ontology is more than just a way to organize data, it’s a live, intelligent model of your world. Understanding the semantic, kinetic, and dynamic layers helps you design systems that are not only accurate and aligned, but also adaptive and powerful.

Whether you’re building a single source of truth or an entire digital twin of your operations, this layered approach is what makes Foundry and Gotham uniquely effective.
